l1: Question 1 — Is the slowness affecting one user or multiple users?
Question 2 — Is this a physical machine or a virtual machine (VM)?
Question 3 — Is it a client OS or server OS?
Question 4 — What is the exact Stop Code and bugcheck parameters shown on the screen or in the event log?
Question 5 — Is the Stop Code always identical, or does it vary with every crash?
Question 6 — How frequently does the bugcheck occur?
Question 7 — Does the crash happen during a specific system state or activity?
Question 8 — Does the machine crash immediately when a specific application or peripheral is used?
Question 9 — Were there any hardware modifications, component upgrades, or BIOS updates applied right before the crashes started?
l2: Has there been a recent Windows Update, third-party software deployment, or graphics driver installation?
Was there a recent update or policy push to your endpoint security or antivirus agent?
Did Windows successfully write a crash dump file (MEMORY.DMP or a minidump) to the disk?
Does the machine reboot immediately after the crash, or does it hang on the BSOD screen indefinitely?
What does Event Viewer report under Event ID 41 (Kernel-Power) or Event ID 1001 (Bugcheck)?

l1: Question 1 — Is the slowness affecting one user or multiple users?
Question 2 — What is the exact Stop Code and text string displayed on the blue screen?
Question 3 — Does the blue screen mention a specific file with a .sys, .dll, or .exe extension at the bottom?
Question 4 — Does the crash screen complete its progress counter to 100% and successfully restart, or does it hang permanently on the blue screen?
Question 5 — Does the machine bugcheck at a consistent point during boot up, or does it only happen after logging into user sessions?
Question 6 — Does the crash occur immediately when performing a specific task or executing a heavy script?
Question 7 — Is the bugcheck accompanied by an audible glitch, screen tearing, or cooling fan speed spikes right before it halts?
l2: If you browse to C:\Windows\, do you see a file named MEMORY.DMP, or are there small .dmp files present in C:\Windows\Minidump\?
Does the system report an Event ID 1001 (Bugcheck) in the System Event Log upon booting back up?
Are there any recurring disk errors, controller warnings (storahci), or WHEA hardware warnings logged in Event Viewer right before the time of the crash?
For Virtual Machines: Does the host hypervisor log an unexpected reset, power loss, or a physical storage disconnect for this VM node?
Has any security agent software (EDR, Antivirus, or DLP) updated its definition files or scanning engine within the last 48 hours?

l1: Recover the Stop Code and Faulting Driver.
Unplug Non-Essential Hardware.
Run the Built-In Windows Memory Diagnostic.
Boot into Safe Mode to Isolate Third-Party Drivers.
Check and Roll Back Recent Updates.
Repair Core Windows Image Binaries.
l2: Perform Deep-Dive Kernel Analysis via WinDbg.
Stress-Test Driver Stability via Driver Verifier.
Run Advanced Memory Validation via MemTest86.

l1: Complete Memory Dumps.
Windows Event Logs.
Collect TSS

steps.action: Locate and confirm the generation of MEMORY.DMP or minidump files in C:\Windows\ or C:\Windows\Minidump\.
steps.expect: Crash dump files are present and match the timestamp of the latest system failure.

steps.action: Query the System Event Log for Event ID 1001 (Bugcheck) or Event ID 41 (Kernel-Power) to recover raw Stop Codes and parameters.
steps.expect: Identify the exact hex error signature (e.g., 0x000000D1) and any named faulting driver file.

steps.action: Open the crash dump file in WinDbg, configure public symbols, and execute the !analyze -v routine.
steps.expect: Isolate the execution stack to find the exact third-party .sys driver or module that triggered the kernel panic.

steps.action: Boot the affected machine into Windows Safe Mode to bypass non-essential startup items and third-party kernel filters.
steps.expect: Observe system stability; if the machine remains stable, physical hardware is healthy and a software driver is the culprit.

steps.action: If dump files are generic, activate Driver Verifier against all non-Microsoft drivers using standard verification flags.
steps.expect: The system intentionally bugchecks upon reboot, explicitly naming the rogue memory-corrupting driver.

steps.action: Uninstall, roll back, or update the specific vendor driver identified by the WinDbg stack analysis or Driver Verifier output.
steps.expect: The unseated, corrupted, or conflicting system module is cleanly replaced or removed from the boot sequence.

steps.action: Execute standard DISM and SFC file integrity scanners via an administrative command prompt to repair core OS binaries.
steps.expect: Any corrupted file structures or core kernel resources are restored to a healthy baseline status.

steps.action: Run verifier /reset to disable any active verifier testing flags, then monitor the machine under normal operational workloads.
steps.expect: The environment processes workflows reliably over an extended monitoring period without generating any further crash telemetry.
